does: This paper presents a method for discovering interpretable circuits in language models using discrete sparse autoencoders trained on positive and negative examples where the model succeeds only on positive cases. The core innovation involves training sparse autoencoders on attention head outputs, then discretizing the learned representations into integer codes to identify which heads participate in specific computations. By measuring the overlap of codes unique to positive examples across heads, the method directly identifies circuit components without requiring expensive ablations or model modifications. The approach is evaluated on three benchmark tasks—indirect object identification, greater-than comparisons, and docstring completion—where it achieves higher precision and recall than existing circuit identification techniques.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of mechanistic interpretability and circuit analysis in neural networks, extending prior research on understanding how transformer models perform computation through distributed attention head interactions. It responds to limitations in existing circuit discovery methods like ablation-based approaches that are computationally expensive and activation patching techniques that require careful architectural modifications. The paper connects to sparse autoencoders developed for feature extraction and interpretability, repurposing them with discretization to create a more scalable alternative. It also builds on theoretical frameworks from prior work analyzing specific tasks like indirect object identification and greater-than comparisons, which have served as testing grounds for interpretability methods.
enables: This method enables systematic circuit discovery at scale, potentially allowing researchers to map computation across many tasks and larger models without prohibitive computational costs. The discretized sparse autoencoder approach could generalize beyond attention heads to other model components, facilitating broader mechanistic understanding of language models. By providing a more reliable circuit identification tool, the work supports downstream applications like targeted model editing, adversarial robustness improvements through circuit manipulation, and development of more interpretable model architectures. The scalability improvements could enable studying how circuits change across model families, scales, and training procedures, potentially revealing general principles of how neural networks organize computations.
